question: According to the Ghost's own account of the murder in the orchard, what poison was poured into King Hamlet's ear, and what did it do to his blood?
expected_answer: Claudius poured the juice of cursed hebenon into his ear; its leperous distilment coursed through his body and curdled his thin and wholesome blood like acid dropped into milk.

required_evidence_quotes.speaker: GHOST
required_evidence_quotes.quote:
With juice of cursed hebenon in a vial,
And in the porches of my ears did pour
The leperous distilment

required_evidence_quotes.speaker: GHOST
required_evidence_quotes.quote:
And curd, like eager droppings into milk,
The thin and wholesome blood.

raw_chunks.scene_title: A more remote part of the Castle.
raw_chunks.text:
With juice of cursed hebenon in a vial,
And in the porches of my ears did pour
The leperous distilment, whose effect
Holds such an enmity with blood of man
That swift as quicksilver it courses through
The natural gates and alleys of the body;
And with a sudden vigour it doth posset
And curd, like eager droppings into milk,
The thin and wholesome blood. So did it mine;
And a most instant tetter bark’d about,
Most lazar-like, with vile and loathsome crust
All my smooth body.
Thus was I, sleeping, by a brother’s hand,
Of life, of crown, of queen at once dispatch’d:
Cut off even in the blossoms of my sin,
Unhous’led, disappointed, unanel’d;
No reckoning made, but sent to my account
With all my imperfections on my head.
O horrible! O horrible! most horrible!
If thou hast nature in thee, bear it not;
Let not the royal bed of Denmark be
A couch for luxury and damned incest.
But howsoever thou pursu’st this

.
You are keen, my lord, you are keen.

HAMLET.
It would cost you a groaning to take off my edge.

OPHELIA.
Still better, and worse.

HAMLET.
So you mistake your husbands.—Begin, murderer. Pox, leave thy damnable
faces, and begin. Come, the croaking raven doth bellow for revenge.

LUCIANUS.
Thoughts black, hands apt, drugs fit, and time agreeing,
Confederate season, else no creature seeing;
Thou mixture rank, of midnight weeds collected,
With Hecate’s ban thrice blasted, thrice infected,
Thy natural magic and dire property
On wholesome life usurp immediately.

[_Pours the poison into the sleeper’s ears._]

HAMLET.
He poisons him i’ th’garden for’s estate. His name’s Gonzago. The story
is extant, and written in very choice Italian. You shall see anon how
the murderer gets the love of Gonzago’s wife.

OPHELIA.
The King rises.

HAMLET.
What, frighted with false fire?

QUEEN.

With juice of cursed hebenon in a vial,
And in the porches of my ears did pour
The leperous distilment, whose effect
Holds such an enmity with blood of man
That swift as quicksilver it courses through
The natural gates and alleys of the body;
And with a sudden vigour it doth posset
And curd, like eager droppings into milk,
The thin and wholesome blood. So did it mine;
And a most instant tetter bark’d about,
Most lazar-like, with vile and loathsome crust
All my smooth body.

Of life, of crown, of queen at once dispatch’d:
Cut off even in the blossoms of my sin,
Unhous’led, disappointed, unanel’d;
No reckoning made, but sent to my account
With all my imperfections on my head.
O horrible! O horrible! most horrible!
If thou hast nature in thee, bear it not;
Let not the royal bed of Denmark be
A couch for luxury and damned incest.
But howsoever thou pursu’st this

question: According to the Ghost's own account of the murder in the orchard, what poison was poured into King Hamlet's ear, and what did it do to his blood?
expected_answer: Claudius poured the juice of cursed hebenon into his ear; its leperous distilment coursed through his body and curdled his thin and wholesome blood like acid dropped into milk.

raw_chunks.scene_title: A more remote part of the Castle.
raw_chunks.text:
SCENE V. A more remote part of the Castle.

Enter Ghost and Hamlet.

HAMLET.
Whither wilt thou lead me? Speak, I’ll go no further.

GHOST.
Mark me.

HAMLET.
I will.

GHOST.
My hour is almost come,
When I to sulph’rous and tormenting flames
Must render up myself.

HAMLET.
Alas, poor ghost!

GHOST.
Pity me not, but lend thy serious hearing
To what I shall unfold.

HAMLET.
Speak, I am bound to hear.

GHOST.
So art thou to revenge, when thou shalt hear.

HAMLET.
What?

GHOST.
I am thy father’s spirit,
Doom’d for a certain term to walk the night,
And for the day confin’d to fast in fires,
Till the foul crimes done in my days of nature
Are burnt and purg’d away. But that I am forbid
To tell the secrets of my prison-house,
I could a tale unfold whose lightest word
Would harrow up thy soul; freeze thy young blood,
Make thy two eyes like stars start

And curd, like eager droppings into milk,
The thin and wholesome blood. So did it mine;
And a most instant tetter bark’d about,
Most lazar-like, with vile and loathsome crust
All my smooth body.
Thus was I, sleeping, by a brother’s hand,
Of life, of crown, of queen at once dispatch’d:
Cut off even in the blossoms of my sin,
Unhous’led, disappointed, unanel’d;
No reckoning made, but sent to my account

O horrible! O horrible! most horrible!

user_prompt:
Context chunks:
[act01_scene05_chunk001 | Act 1 Scene 5 | A more remote part of the Castle. | 256 tokens]
SCENE V. A more remote part of the Castle.

Enter Ghost and Hamlet.

HAMLET.
Whither wilt thou lead me? Speak, I’ll go no further.

GHOST.
Mark me.

HAMLET.
I will.

GHOST.
My hour is almost come,
When I to sulph’rous and tormenting flames
Must render up myself.

HAMLET.
Alas, poor ghost!

GHOST.
Pity me not, but lend thy serious hearing
To what I shall unfold.

HAMLET.
Speak, I am bound to hear.

GHOST.
So art thou to revenge, when thou shalt hear.

HAMLET.
What?

GHOST.
I am thy father’s spirit,
Doom’d for a certain term to walk the night,
And for the day confin’d to fast in fires,
Till the foul crimes done in my days of nature
Are burnt and purg’d away. But that I am forbid
To tell the secrets of my prison-house,
I could a tale unfold whose lightest word
Would harrow up thy soul; freeze thy young blood,
Make thy two eyes like stars start

[act01_scene05_chunk005 | Act 1 Scene 5 | A more remote part of the Castle. | 256 tokens]
With juice of cursed hebenon in a vial,
And in the porches of my ears did pour
The leperous distilment, whose effect
Holds such an enmity with blood of man
That swift as quicksilver it courses through
The natural gates and alleys of the body;
And with a sudden vigour it doth posset
And curd, like eager droppings into milk,
The thin and wholesome blood. So did it mine;
And a most instant tetter bark’d about,
Most lazar-like, with vile and loathsome crust
All my smooth body.
Thus was I, sleeping, by a brother’s hand,
Of life, of crown, of queen at once dispatch’d:
Cut off even in the blossoms of my sin,
Unhous’led, disappointed, unanel’d;
No reckoning made, but sent to my account
With all my imperfections on my head.
O horrible! O horrible! most horrible!
If thou hast nature in thee, bear it not;
Let not the royal bed of Denmark be
A couch for luxury and damned incest.
But howsoever thou pursu’st this

question: According to the Ghost's own account of the murder in the orchard, what poison was poured into King Hamlet's ear, and what did it do to his blood?

question: According to the Ghost's own account of the murder in the orchard, what poison was poured into King Hamlet's ear, and what did it do to his blood?
expected_answer: Claudius poured the juice of cursed hebenon into his ear; its leperous distilment coursed through his body and curdled his thin and wholesome blood like acid dropped into milk.

.
You are keen, my lord, you are keen.

HAMLET.
It would cost you a groaning to take off my edge.

OPHELIA.
Still better, and worse.

HAMLET.
So you mistake your husbands.—Begin, murderer. Pox, leave thy damnable
faces, and begin. Come, the croaking raven doth bellow for revenge.

LUCIANUS.
Thoughts black, hands apt, drugs fit, and time agreeing,
Confederate season, else no creature seeing;
Thou mixture rank, of midnight weeds collected,
With Hecate’s ban thrice blasted, thrice infected,
Thy natural magic and dire property
On wholesome life usurp immediately.

[_Pours the poison into the sleeper’s ears._]

HAMLET.
He poisons him i’ th’garden for’s estate. His name’s Gonzago. The story
is extant, and written in very choice Italian. You shall see anon how
the murderer gets the love of Gonzago’s wife.

OPHELIA.
The King rises.

HAMLET.
What, frighted with false fire?

QUEEN.

Thy natural magic and dire property
On wholesome life usurp immediately.

[_Pours the poison into the sleeper’s ears._]

HAMLET.
He poisons him i’ th’garden for’s estate. His name’s Gonzago. The story
is extant, and written in very choice Italian. You shall see anon how
the murderer gets the love of Gonzago’s wife.

OPHELIA.
The King rises.

HAMLET.
What, frighted with false fire?

QUEEN.

raw_chunks.text:
o’er with the pale cast of thought,
And enterprises of great pith and moment,
With this regard their currents turn awry
And lose the name of action. Soft you now,
The fair Ophelia! Nymph, in thy orisons
Be all my sins remember’d.

OPHELIA.
Good my lord,
How does your honour for this many a day?

HAMLET.
I humbly thank you; well, well, well.

OPHELIA.
My lord, I have remembrances of yours
That I have longed long to re-deliver.
I pray you, now receive them.

HAMLET.
No, not I.
I never gave you aught.

OPHELIA.
My honour’d lord, you know right well you did,
And with them words of so sweet breath compos’d
As made the things more rich; their perfume lost,
Take these again; for to the noble mind
Rich gifts wax poor when givers prove unkind.
There, my lord.

HAMLET.
Ha, ha! Are you honest?

OPHELIA.
My lord?

HAMLET.
Are you fair

raw_chunks.scene_title: Elsinore. A room in the Castle.
raw_chunks.text:
By Gis and by Saint Charity,
     Alack, and fie for shame!
   Young men will do’t if they come to’t;
     By Cock, they are to blame.

   Quoth she, before you tumbled me,
     You promis’d me to wed.
   So would I ha’ done, by yonder sun,
     An thou hadst not come to my bed.

KING.
How long hath she been thus?

OPHELIA.
I hope all will be well. We must be patient. But I cannot choose but
weep, to think they would lay him i’ th’ cold ground. My brother shall
know of it. And so I thank you for your good counsel. Come, my coach!
Good night, ladies; good night, sweet ladies; good night, good night.

[_Exit._]

KING.
Follow her close; give her good watch, I pray you.

[_Exit Horatio._]

O, this is the poison of deep grief; it springs
All from her father’s death. O Gertrude, Gertrude,
When sorrows come, they come not single spies,
But in battal

question: Why does Claudius summon Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to court, what task involving England are they later given, and what finally happens to them?
expected_answer: Claudius summons them because of Hamlet's transformation, to spend time with Hamlet and uncover what afflicts him; later Claudius dispatches them with a commission to escort Hamlet to England; in the end they are executed, as the English ambassadors report that Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead.

raw_chunks.text:
SCENE II. A room in the Castle.

Enter King, Queen, Rosencrantz, Guildenstern and Attendants.

KING.
Welcome, dear Rosencrantz and Guildenstern.
Moreover that we much did long to see you,
The need we have to use you did provoke
Our hasty sending. Something have you heard
Of Hamlet’s transformation; so I call it,
Since nor th’exterior nor the inward man
Resembles that it was. What it should be,
More than his father’s death, that thus hath put him
So much from th’understanding of himself,
I cannot dream of. I entreat you both
That, being of so young days brought up with him,
And since so neighbour’d to his youth and humour,
That you vouchsafe your rest here in our court
Some little time, so by your companies
To draw him on to pleasures and to gather,
So much as from occasion you may glean,
Whether aught to us unknown afflicts him thus
That, open’d, lies within our remedy.

QUEEN.
Good gentlemen, he hath much talk’d of you,
And

KING.
Follow him at foot. Tempt him with speed aboard;
Delay it not; I’ll have him hence tonight.
Away, for everything is seal’d and done
That else leans on th’affair. Pray you make haste.

[_Exeunt Rosencrantz and Guildenstern._]

And England, if my love thou hold’st at aught,—
As my great power thereof may give thee sense,
Since yet thy cicatrice looks raw and red
After the Danish sword, and thy free awe
Pays homage to us,—thou mayst not coldly set
Our sovereign process, which imports at full,
By letters conjuring to that effect,
The present death of Hamlet. Do it, England;
For like the hectic in my blood he rages,
And thou must cure me. Till I know ’tis done,
Howe’er my haps, my joys were ne’er begun.

[_Exit._]

I your commission will forthwith dispatch,

That Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead.

model_output:
Based on the provided context, here are the answers to your questions:

**Why Claudius summons them:**
Claudius summons Rosencrantz and Guildenstern because he has heard of "Hamlet's transformation," noting that neither his exterior nor his inward self resembles who he was before. Claudius cannot determine what caused this change other than Hamlet's father's death. He asks them to stay at court for a little while to "draw him on to pleasures" and gather information ("glean") to see if anything unknown is afflicting Hamlet that could be remedied.
*   **Evidence:** `act02_scene02_chunk001`

**The task involving England:**
Later, Claudius instructs Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to follow Hamlet and tempt him to board a ship immediately so he can be taken away that night. Once Hamlet is gone, Claudius sends a letter to England commanding them to execute Hamlet ("The present death of Hamlet").
*   **Evidence:** `act04_scene03_chunk005`

**What finally happens to them:**
The provided context **does not answer** what finally happens to Rosencrantz and Guildenstern. While the text shows them being sent to England to carry out the order to kill Hamlet, it does not describe their ultimate fate.

Context chunks:
[act02_scene02_chunk001 | Act 2 Scene 2 | A room in the Castle. | 256 tokens]
SCENE II. A room in the Castle.

Enter King, Queen, Rosencrantz, Guildenstern and Attendants.

KING.
Welcome, dear Rosencrantz and Guildenstern.
Moreover that we much did long to see you,
The need we have to use you did provoke
Our hasty sending. Something have you heard
Of Hamlet’s transformation; so I call it,
Since nor th’exterior nor the inward man
Resembles that it was. What it should be,
More than his father’s death, that thus hath put him
So much from th’understanding of himself,
I cannot dream of. I entreat you both
That, being of so young days brought up with him,
And since so neighbour’d to his youth and humour,
That you vouchsafe your rest here in our court
Some little time, so by your companies
To draw him on to pleasures and to gather,
So much as from occasion you may glean,
Whether aught to us unknown afflicts him thus
That, open’d, lies within our remedy.

QUEEN.
Good gentlemen, he hath much talk’d of you,
And

[act04_scene03_chunk005 | Act 4 Scene 3 | Another room in the Castle. | 211 tokens]
KING.
Follow him at foot. Tempt him with speed aboard;
Delay it not; I’ll have him hence tonight.
Away, for everything is seal’d and done
That else leans on th’affair. Pray you make haste.

[_Exeunt Rosencrantz and Guildenstern._]

And England, if my love thou hold’st at aught,—
As my great power thereof may give thee sense,
Since yet thy cicatrice looks raw and red
After the Danish sword, and thy free awe
Pays homage to us,—thou mayst not coldly set
Our sovereign process, which imports at full,
By letters conjuring to that effect,
The present death of Hamlet. Do it, England;
For like the hectic in my blood he rages,
And thou must cure me. Till I know ’tis done,
Howe’er my haps, my joys were ne’er begun.

[_Exit._]

model_output:
Based on the provided context, here are the answers to your questions:

**Why Claudius summons them:**
Claudius summons Rosencrantz and Guildenstern because he has heard of "Hamlet's transformation," noting that neither his exterior nor his inward self resembles who he was before. Claudius cannot determine what caused this change other than Hamlet's father's death. He asks them to stay at court for a little while to "draw him on to pleasures" and gather information ("glean") to see if anything unknown is afflicting Hamlet that could be remedied.
*   **Evidence:** `act02_scene02_chunk001`

**The task involving England:**
Later, Claudius instructs Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to follow Hamlet and tempt him to board a ship immediately so he can be taken away that night. Once Hamlet is gone, Claudius sends a letter to England commanding them to execute Hamlet ("The present death of Hamlet").
*   **Evidence:** `act04_scene03_chunk005`

**What finally happens to them:**
The provided context **does not answer** what finally happens to Rosencrantz and Guildenstern. While the text shows them being sent to England to carry out the order to kill Hamlet, it does not describe their ultimate fate.

question: Why does Claudius summon Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to court, what task involving England are they later given, and what finally happens to them?
expected_answer: Claudius summons them because of Hamlet's transformation, to spend time with Hamlet and uncover what afflicts him; later Claudius dispatches them with a commission to escort Hamlet to England; in the end they are executed, as the English ambassadors report that Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead.

model_output:
Based on the provided context, here are the answers to your questions:

**Why Claudius summons them:**
Claudius summons Rosencrantz and Guildenstern because Hamlet's behavior has changed drastically ("Hamlet's transformation"), and he does not understand the cause ("What it should be... I cannot dream of"). He believes that because they were raised with Hamlet and are close to his age and nature, they are best suited to find out what is troubling him. The King asks them to stay at court to "draw him on to pleasures" and to discover "Whether aught to us unknown afflicts him thus That, open'd, lies within our remedy."
*   **Evidence:** `act02_scene02_chunk001`

**The task involving England:**
Later in the play, Claudius gives them a secret order to take Hamlet to England. He instructs them to "Tempt him with speed aboard" and ensure he leaves that night. Once Hamlet is gone, Claudius sends a letter to the King of England commanding that Hamlet be executed ("The present death of Hamlet").
*   **Evidence:** `act04_scene03_chunk005`

**What finally happens to them:**
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are killed while in England. An ambassador from England arrives to report that their command to execute Hamlet has been fulfilled, and consequently, Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead. Hamlet never ordered their death; it was a result of the circumstances ("so jump upon this bloody question").
*   **Evidence:** `act05_scene02_chunk027`

**Why Claudius summons them:**

I cannot dream of"). He believes that because they were raised with Hamlet and are close to his age and nature, they are best suited to find out what is troubling him. The King asks them to stay at court to "draw him on to pleasures" and to discover "Whether aught to us unknown afflicts him thus That, open'd, lies within our remedy."
*   **Evidence:** `act02_scene02_chunk001`

**The task involving England:**
Later in the play, Claudius gives them a secret order to take Hamlet to England. He instructs them to "Tempt him with speed aboard" and ensure he leaves that night. Once Hamlet is gone, Claudius sends a letter to the King of England commanding that Hamlet be executed ("The present death of Hamlet").
*   **Evidence:** `act04_scene03_chunk005`

**What finally happens to them:**
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are killed while in England. An ambassador from England arrives to report that their command to execute Hamlet has been fulfilled, and consequently, Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead. Hamlet never ordered their death; it was a result of the circumstances ("so jump upon this bloody question").
*   **Evidence:** `act05_scene02_chunk027`

question: Why does Claudius summon Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to court, what task involving England are they later given, and what finally happens to them?

raw_chunks.text:
SCENE II. A room in the Castle.

Enter King, Queen, Rosencrantz, Guildenstern and Attendants.

KING.
Welcome, dear Rosencrantz and Guildenstern.
Moreover that we much did long to see you,
The need we have to use you did provoke
Our hasty sending. Something have you heard
Of Hamlet’s transformation; so I call it,
Since nor th’exterior nor the inward man
Resembles that it was. What it should be,
More than his father’s death, that thus hath put him
So much from th’understanding of himself,
I cannot dream of. I entreat you both
That, being of so young days brought up with him,
And since so neighbour’d to his youth and humour,
That you vouchsafe your rest here in our court
Some little time, so by your companies
To draw him on to pleasures and to gather,
So much as from occasion you may glean,
Whether aught to us unknown afflicts him thus
That, open’d, lies within our remedy.

QUEEN.
Good gentlemen, he hath much talk’d of you,
And

see?
If aught of woe or wonder, cease your search.

FORTINBRAS.
This quarry cries on havoc. O proud death,
What feast is toward in thine eternal cell,
That thou so many princes at a shot
So bloodily hast struck?

FIRST AMBASSADOR.
The sight is dismal;
And our affairs from England come too late.
The ears are senseless that should give us hearing,
To tell him his commandment is fulfill’d,
That Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead.
Where should we have our thanks?

HORATIO.
Not from his mouth,
Had it th’ability of life to thank you.
He never gave commandment for their death.
But since, so jump upon this bloody question,
You from the Polack wars, and you from England
Are here arriv’d, give order that these bodies
High on a stage be placed to the view,
And let me speak to th’ yet unknowing world
How these things came about. So shall you hear
Of carnal, bloody and unnatural acts,
Of accidental judgements, casual slaughters,
Of deaths put on by cunning and

model_output:
Based on the provided context, here are the answers to your questions:

**Why Claudius summons them:**
Claudius summons Rosencrantz and Guildenstern because he has heard of "Hamlet's transformation," noting that neither his exterior nor his inward self resembles who he was before. Claudius cannot determine what caused this change other than Hamlet's father's death. He asks them to stay at court for a little while to "draw him on to pleasures" and gather information ("glean") from the situation to find out "Whether aught to us unknown afflicts him thus" so that a remedy can be found.
*   **Evidence:** `act02_scene02_chunk001`

**What task involving England they are later given:**
The provided context does not contain information regarding a specific task involving England that Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are given. While the text mentions ambassadors arriving from England, it does not detail the orders sent to Rosencrantz and Guildenstern while they were alive.
*   **Evidence:** None found in the provided chunks.

**What finally happens to them:**
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead. An ambassador reports that their commandment from England has been fulfilled, resulting in their deaths. Horatio clarifies that Hamlet never gave a commandment for their death, but rather they died as a result of the "carnal, bloody and unnatural acts" and "cunning" deaths described in the events leading up to the current scene.
*   **Evidence:** `act05_scene02_chunk027`

question: Why does Claudius summon Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to court, what task involving England are they later given, and what finally happens to them?
expected_answer: Claudius summons them because of Hamlet's transformation, to spend time with Hamlet and uncover what afflicts him; later Claudius dispatches them with a commission to escort Hamlet to England; in the end they are executed, as the English ambassadors report that Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead.

model_output:
Based on the provided context, here are the answers to your questions:

**Why Claudius summons them:**
Claius summons Rosencrantz and Guildenstern because Hamlet's behavior has changed significantly ("Hamlet's transformation"), and neither his outward appearance nor his inner self resembles who he was before his father's death. Claudius asks them to stay at court for a little while to try to draw Hamlet out and discover "Whether aught to us unknown afflicts him thus" so that a remedy can be found.
*   **Evidence:** `[act02_scene02_chunk001]` ("Something have you heard / Of Hamlet's transformation... I entreat you both / That, being of so young days brought up with him... To draw him on to pleasures and to gather... Whether aught to us unknown afflicts him thus")

**What task involving England they are later given:**
The provided context **does not** state what specific task involving England Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were given. While the text mentions ambassadors arriving from England, it does not describe the orders sent to Rosencrantz and Guildenstern regarding England.
*   **Evidence:** The context mentions ambassadors from England arriving to report on the commandment being fulfilled, but does not detail the content of the commandment given to Rosencrantz and Guildenstern. (`[act05_scene02_chunk027]`)

**What finally happens to them:**
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead. An ambassador reports that their commandment from England has been fulfilled, resulting in their deaths. Hamlet never gave the commandment for their death; it was a result of the "carnal, bloody and unnatural acts" and "deaths put on by cunning" that Hamlet later intends to explain to the world.
*   **Evidence:** `[act05_scene02_chunk027]` ("That Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead... He never gave commandment for their death... Of deaths put on by cunning")

the foul crimes done in my days of nature

To ears of flesh and blood. List, list, O, list!

Before mine uncle. I’ll observe his looks;

model_output:
Hamlet tests the Ghost's accusation by having actors perform a play that mirrors the murder of his father, intending to observe Claudius's reaction to confirm the truth.

Evidence from the context:
*   In **act02_scene02_chunk042**, Hamlet states his plan: "I'll have these players Play something like the murder of my father Before mine uncle. I'll observe his looks; I'll tent him to the quick." He further explains his reasoning: "The spirit that I have seen May be the devil... I'll have grounds More relative than this. The play's the thing Wherein I'll catch the conscience of the King."
*   In **act03_scene02_chunk017** and **act03_scene02_chunk018**, Hamlet introduces the play to the King as "The Mousetrap," describing it as "the image of a murder done in Vienna," and urges the performance to begin ("Begin, murderer").

Chunk IDs: act02_scene02_chunk042, act03_scene02_chunk017, act03_scene02_chunk018

model_output:
Hamlet plans to test the Ghost's accusation by having actors perform a play that resembles the murder of his father. He intends to observe King Claudius's reaction during this performance, believing that the play will reveal the King's guilty conscience.

**Evidence:**
*   **act02_scene02_chunk042**: Hamlet states, "I'll have these players Play something like the murder of my father Before mine uncle. I'll observe his looks; I'll tent him to the quick." He further explains his reasoning: "The spirit that I have seen May be the devil... I'll have grounds More relative than this. The play's the thing Wherein I'll catch the conscience of the King."
*   **act01_scene05_chunk002**: This chunk provides the context of the accusation itself, where the Ghost commands Hamlet to "Revenge his foul and most unnatural murder," which is the specific claim Hamlet intends to verify.

**Answer:**
Hamlet tests the truth of the accusation by staging a play depicting his father's murder and observing Claudius's reaction, hoping to "catch the conscience of the King."

unnatural.

HAMLET.
Haste me to know’t, that I, with wings as swift
As meditation or the thoughts of love
May sweep to my revenge.

GHOST.
I find thee apt;
And duller shouldst thou be than the fat weed
That rots itself in ease on Lethe wharf,
Wouldst thou not stir in this. Now, Hamlet, hear.
’Tis given out that, sleeping in my orchard,
A serpent stung me; so the whole ear of Denmark
Is by a forged process of my death
Rankly abus’d; but know, thou noble youth,
The serpent that did sting thy father’s life
Now wears his crown.

HAMLET.
O my prophetic soul!
Mine uncle!

GHOST.
Ay, that incestuous, that adulterate beast,
With witchcraft of his wit, with traitorous gifts,—
O wicked wit, and gifts, that have the power
So to seduce!—won to his shameful lust
The will of my most seeming-virtuous queen.
O Hamlet, what a falling off was there,
From me, whose love was of that dignity
That

the scene,
Been struck so to the soul that presently
They have proclaim’d their malefactions.
For murder, though it have no tongue, will speak
With most miraculous organ. I’ll have these players
Play something like the murder of my father
Before mine uncle. I’ll observe his looks;
I’ll tent him to the quick. If he but blench,
I know my course. The spirit that I have seen
May be the devil, and the devil hath power
T’assume a pleasing shape, yea, and perhaps
Out of my weakness and my melancholy,
As he is very potent with such spirits,
Abuses me to damn me. I’ll have grounds
More relative than this. The play’s the thing
Wherein I’ll catch the conscience of the King.

[_Exit._]

In my heart’s core, ay, in my heart of heart,
As I do thee. Something too much of this.
There is a play tonight before the King.
One scene of it comes near the circumstance
Which I have told thee, of my father’s death.
I prithee, when thou see’st that act a-foot,
Even with the very comment of thy soul
Observe mine uncle. If his occulted guilt
Do not itself unkennel in one speech,
It is a damned ghost that we have seen;
And my imaginations are as foul
As Vulcan’s stithy. Give him heedful note;
For I mine eyes will rivet to his face;
And after we will both our judgements join
In censure of his seeming.

HORATIO.
Well, my lord.
If he steal aught the whilst this play is playing,
And ’scape detecting, I will pay

have well, and it destroy!
Both here and hence pursue me lasting strife,
If, once a widow, ever I be wife.

HAMLET.
[_To Ophelia._] If she should break it now.

PLAYER KING.
’Tis deeply sworn. Sweet, leave me here awhile.
My spirits grow dull, and fain I would beguile
The tedious day with sleep.
[_Sleeps._]

PLAYER QUEEN.
Sleep rock thy brain,
And never come mischance between us twain.

[_Exit._]

HAMLET.
Madam, how like you this play?

QUEEN.
The lady protests too much, methinks.

HAMLET.
O, but she’ll keep her word.

KING.
Have you heard the argument? Is there no offence in’t?

HAMLET.
No, no, they do but jest, poison in jest; no offence i’ th’ world.

KING.
What do you call the play?

HAMLET.
_The Mousetrap._ Marry, how? Tropically. This play is the image of a

Hamlet tests whether the Ghost's accusation against Claudius is true by having actors perform a play that reenacts the murder of his father. He plans to observe Claudius's reaction during the performance; if the King shows guilt (such as blenching or speaking out), it will confirm the Ghost's story. Hamlet explicitly states his intention to "catch the conscience of the King" through this method.

**Evidence from context:**
*   **act02_scene02_chunk042**: Hamlet says, "I'll have these players Play something like the murder of my father Before mine uncle. I'll observe his looks; I'll tent him to the quick. If he but blench, I know my course." He also states, "The play's the thing / Wherein I'll catch the conscience of the King."
*   **act03_scene02_chunk006**: Hamlet instructs Horatio, "There is a play tonight before the King. One scene of it comes near the circumstance / Which I have told thee, of my father's death... Observe mine uncle. If his occulted guilt / Do not itself unkennel in one speech..."

raw_chunks.text:
SCENE V. A more remote part of the Castle.

Enter Ghost and Hamlet.

HAMLET.
Whither wilt thou lead me? Speak, I’ll go no further.

GHOST.
Mark me.

HAMLET.
I will.

GHOST.
My hour is almost come,
When I to sulph’rous and tormenting flames
Must render up myself.

HAMLET.
Alas, poor ghost!

GHOST.
Pity me not, but lend thy serious hearing
To what I shall unfold.

HAMLET.
Speak, I am bound to hear.

GHOST.
So art thou to revenge, when thou shalt hear.

HAMLET.
What?

GHOST.
I am thy father’s spirit,
Doom’d for a certain term to walk the night,
And for the day confin’d to fast in fires,
Till the foul crimes done in my days of nature
Are burnt and purg’d away. But that I am forbid
To tell the secrets of my prison-house,
I could a tale unfold whose lightest word
Would harrow up thy soul; freeze thy young blood,
Make thy two eyes like stars start

the scene,

Play something like the murder of my father
Before mine uncle. I’ll observe his looks;
I’ll tent him to the quick. If he but blench,
I know my course. The spirit that I have seen
May be the devil, and the devil hath power
T’assume a pleasing shape, yea, and perhaps
Out of my weakness and my melancholy,
As he is very potent with such spirits,
Abuses me to damn me. I’ll have grounds
More relative than this. The play’s the thing
Wherein I’ll catch the conscience of the King.

[_Exit._]

ROSENCRANTZ.

We o’er-raught on the way. Of these we told him,

To hear of it. They are about the court,
And, as I think, they have already order
This night to play before him.

POLONIUS.
’Tis most true;
And he beseech’d me to entreat your Majesties
To hear and see the matter.

KING.
With all my heart; and it doth much content me
To hear him so inclin’d.
Good gentlemen, give him a further edge,
And drive his purpose on to these delights.

ROSENCRANTZ.
We shall, my lord.

[_Exeunt Rosencrantz and Guildenstern._]

KING.
Sweet Gertrude, leave us too,
For we have closely sent for Hamlet hither,

model_output:
Hamlet tests whether the Ghost's accusation is true by having actors perform a play that mirrors the murder of his father. He intends to observe King Claudius's reaction during the performance; if the King shows any sign of guilt (such as flinching), Hamlet will know the spirit's story is true. He explicitly states his plan to "catch the conscience of the King" through this method.

**Evidence:**
*   **act02_scene02_chunk042**: Hamlet declares, "I'll have these players Play something like the murder of my father Before mine uncle. I'll observe his looks; I'll tent him to the quick. If he but blench, I know my course." He also states, "The play's the thing / Wherein I'll catch the conscience of the King."
*   **act03_scene02_chunk018**: Hamlet refers to the play as "_The Mousetrap_" and describes it as "the image of a murder done in Vienna," instructing the actors to begin the performance.

[_Sleeps._]

Madam, how like you this play?

model_output:
Hamlet tests whether the Ghost's accusation against Claudius is true by having actors perform a play that mirrors the murder of his father. He intends to observe Claudius's reaction during the performance; if the King shows any sign of guilt (such as flinching or blenching), Hamlet will know the spirit's story is true. He explicitly states his plan to "catch the conscience of the King" through this method, acknowledging that the Ghost might be a devil trying to deceive him, so he needs "grounds more relative" than the ghost's word alone.

**Evidence:**
*   **act02_scene02_chunk042**: Hamlet says, "I'll have these players Play something like the murder of my father Before mine uncle. I'll observe his looks; I'll tent him to the quick. If he but blench, I know my course." He also notes, "The play's the thing / Wherein I'll catch the conscience of the King."
*   **act03_scene02_chunk017**: Hamlet refers to the play as "_The Mousetrap_."

question: How does Hamlet test whether the Ghost's accusation against Claudius is true?
expected_answer: He arranges a play resembling his father's murder, watches Claudius's reaction, and treats Claudius's disturbed response as confirmation.

the scene,
Been struck so to the soul that presently
They have proclaim’d their malefactions.
For murder, though it have no tongue, will speak
With most miraculous organ. I’ll have these players
Play something like the murder of my father
Before mine uncle. I’ll observe his looks;
I’ll tent him to the quick. If he but blench,
I know my course. The spirit that I have seen
May be the devil, and the devil hath power
T’assume a pleasing shape, yea, and perhaps
Out of my weakness and my melancholy,
As he is very potent with such spirits,
Abuses me to damn me. I’ll have grounds
More relative than this. The play’s the thing
Wherein I’ll catch the conscience of the King.

[_Exit._]




ACT III

[act03_scene02_chunk006 | Act 3 Scene 2 | A hall in the Castle. | 256 tokens]
equal thanks. And blessed are those
Whose blood and judgement are so well co-mingled
That they are not a pipe for Fortune’s finger
To sound what stop she please. Give me that man
That is not passion’s slave, and I will wear him
In my heart’s core, ay, in my heart of heart,
As I do thee. Something too much of this.
There is a play tonight before the King.
One scene of it comes near the circumstance
Which I have told thee, of my father’s death.
I prithee, when thou see’st that act a-foot,
Even with the very comment of thy soul
Observe mine uncle. If his occulted guilt
Do not itself unkennel in one speech,
It is a damned ghost that we have seen;
And my imaginations are as foul
As Vulcan’s stithy. Give him heedful note;
For I mine eyes will rivet to his face;

have well, and it destroy!

If, once a widow, ever I be wife.

HAMLET.

PLAYER KING.
’Tis deeply sworn. Sweet, leave me here awhile.
My spirits grow dull, and fain I would beguile
The tedious day with sleep.
[_Sleeps._]

PLAYER QUEEN.
Sleep rock thy brain,
And never come mischance between us twain.

[_Exit._]

HAMLET.
Madam, how like you this play?

QUEEN.
The lady protests too much, methinks.

HAMLET.
O, but she’ll keep her word.

KING.
Have you heard the argument? Is there no offence in’t?

HAMLET.
No, no, they do but jest, poison in jest; no offence i’ th’ world.

KING.

murder done in Vienna. Gonzago is the Duke

jest, poison in jest; no offence i’ th’ world.

model_output:
Hamlet tests whether the Ghost's accusation is true by having actors perform a play that reenacts the murder of his father. He intends to observe King Claudius's reaction during the performance; if Claudius shows guilt (such as flinching or speaking out), it will confirm the Ghost's story. Hamlet explicitly states, "The play's the thing / Wherein I'll catch the conscience of the King."

**Evidence:**
*   **act02_scene02_chunk042**: Hamlet says, "I'll have these players / Play something like the murder of my father / Before mine uncle. I'll observe his looks... The play's the thing / Wherein I'll catch the conscience of the King."
*   **act03_scene02_chunk006**: Hamlet instructs Horatio, "There is a play tonight before the King. One scene of it comes near the circumstance / Which I have told thee, of my father's death... If his occulted guilt / Do not itself unkennel in one speech..."

model_output:
Hamlet tests the Ghost's accusation by staging a play called "The Mousetrap," which is described as "the image of a murder done in Vienna." He intends for this performance to reveal the truth about the murder, noting that the play is "tropically" related to the event he suspects.

**Evidence:**
*   **act03_scene02_chunk017**: Hamlet asks the King, "Have you heard the argument? Is there no offence in't?" and later names the play "_The Mousetrap_," stating, "This play is the image of a murder done in Vienna."
*   **act03_scene02_chunk018**: Hamlet explains the play is "tropically" connected to the murder and tells the actors to "Begin, murderer."

**Answer:**
Hamlet tests the accusation by having actors perform a play ("The Mousetrap") that mirrors the murder he believes Claudius committed, hoping the King's reaction will confirm the Ghost's story.

question: Trace young Fortinbras's military campaign in order: what was his original aim, how did old Norway redirect it, and what is his army doing when it crosses Denmark?
expected_answer: First Fortinbras gathered lawless resolutes to recover by force the lands his father lost to King Hamlet; then old Norway rebuked him, and gave him three thousand crowns and a commission to employ those soldiers against Poland instead; finally his army marches across Denmark under a promised safe-conduct on its way to Poland.

required_evidence_quotes.speaker: HORATIO
required_evidence_quotes.quote:
But to recover of us by strong hand
And terms compulsatory, those foresaid lands
So by his father lost.

required_evidence_quotes.speaker: VOLTEMAND
required_evidence_quotes.quote:
And his commission to employ those soldiers
So levied as before, against the Polack:

required_evidence_quotes.speaker: FORTINBRAS
required_evidence_quotes.quote:
Craves the conveyance of a promis’d march
Over his kingdom.

raw_chunks.scene_title: Elsinore. A platform before the Castle.
raw_chunks.text:
And carriage of the article design’d,
His fell to Hamlet. Now, sir, young Fortinbras,
Of unimproved mettle, hot and full,
Hath in the skirts of Norway, here and there,
Shark’d up a list of lawless resolutes,
For food and diet, to some enterprise
That hath a stomach in’t; which is no other,
As it doth well appear unto our state,
But to recover of us by strong hand
And terms compulsatory, those foresaid lands
So by his father lost. And this, I take it,
Is the main motive of our preparations,
The source of this our watch, and the chief head
Of this post-haste and rummage in the land.

BARNARDO.
I think it be no other but e’en so:
Well may it sort that this portentous figure
Comes armed through our watch so like the King
That was and is the question of these wars.

HORATIO.
A mote it is to trouble the mind’s eye.
In the most high and palmy state of Rome,
A little ere the mightiest Julius fell,
The graves stood tenant

SCENE IV. A plain in Denmark.

Enter Fortinbras and Forces marching.

FORTINBRAS.
Go, Captain, from me greet the Danish king.
Tell him that by his license, Fortinbras
Craves the conveyance of a promis’d march
Over his kingdom. You know the rendezvous.
If that his Majesty would aught with us,
We shall express our duty in his eye;
And let him know so.

CAPTAIN.
I will do’t, my lord.

FORTINBRAS.
Go softly on.

[_Exeunt all but the Captain._]

Enter Hamlet, Rosencrantz, Guildenstern &c.

HAMLET.
Good sir, whose powers are these?

CAPTAIN.
They are of Norway, sir.

HAMLET.
How purpos’d, sir, I pray you?

CAPTAIN.
Against some part of Poland.

HAMLET.
Who commands them, sir?

CAPTAIN.
The nephew to old Norway, Fortinbras.

HAMLET.
Goes it against the main of Poland, sir,
Or for some frontier?

CAPTAIN

SCENE IV. A plain in Denmark.

Enter Fortinbras and Forces marching.

FORTINBRAS.
Go, Captain, from me greet the Danish king.
Tell him that by his license, Fortinbras
Craves the conveyance of a promis’d march
Over his kingdom. You know the rendezvous.
If that his Majesty would aught with us,
We shall express our duty in his eye;
And let him know so.

CAPTAIN.
I will do’t, my lord.

FORTINBRAS.
Go softly on.

[_Exeunt all but the Captain._]

Enter Hamlet, Rosencrantz, Guildenstern &c.

HAMLET.
Good sir, whose powers are these?

CAPTAIN.
They are of Norway, sir.

HAMLET.
How purpos’d, sir, I pray you?

CAPTAIN.
Against some part of Poland.

HAMLET.
Who commands them, sir?

CAPTAIN.
The nephew to old Norway, Fortinbras.

HAMLET.
Goes it against the main of Poland, sir,
Or for some frontier?

CAPTAIN

[act04_scene04_chunk001 | Act 4 Scene 4 | A plain in Denmark. | 256 tokens]
SCENE IV. A plain in Denmark.

Enter Fortinbras and Forces marching.

FORTINBRAS.
Go, Captain, from me greet the Danish king.
Tell him that by his license, Fortinbras
Craves the conveyance of a promis’d march
Over his kingdom. You know the rendezvous.
If that his Majesty would aught with us,
We shall express our duty in his eye;
And let him know so.

I will do’t, my lord.

FORTINBRAS.
Go softly on.

Good sir, whose powers are these?

CAPTAIN.
They are of Norway, sir.

HAMLET.
How purpos’d, sir, I pray you?

CAPTAIN.
Against some part of Poland.

HAMLET.
Who commands them, sir?

CAPTAIN.
The nephew to old Norway, Fortinbras.

HAMLET.
Goes it against the main of Poland, sir,
Or for some frontier?

CAPTAIN

model_output:
Based on the provided context, here is the trace of young Fortinbras's military campaign:

1.  **Original Aim:** Fortinbras originally raised levies (soldiers) with the aim of preparing against Poland. However, upon closer inspection, it was discovered that his true target was the King of Denmark ("your Highness").
    *   *Evidence:* `[act02_scene02_chunk005]` ("...preparation 'gainst the Polack; / But better look'd into, he truly found / It was against your Highness...")

2.  **Redirection by Old Norway:** When the King of Norway (Fortinbras's uncle) learned of this, he sent out arrests against Fortinbras. Fortinbras obeyed, received a rebuke, and vowed never to attack the Danish King again. Consequently, old Norway redirected the soldiers to fight against Poland instead.
    *   *Evidence:* `[act02_scene02_chunk005]` ("...sends out arrests / On Fortinbras; which he, in brief, obeys, / Receives rebuke from Norway; and in fine, / Makes vow before his uncle never more / To give th'assay of arms against your Majesty. / ... Gives him three thousand crowns in annual fee, / And his commission to employ those soldiers / So levied as before, against the Polack")

3.  **Current Activity Crossing Denmark:** When Fortinbras's army crosses Denmark, they are marching to a specific rendezvous point to convey a request to the Danish King for permission to march over his kingdom.
    *   *Evidence:* `[act04_scene04_chunk001]` ("...Craves the conveyance of a promis'd march / Over his kingdom. You know the rendezvous.")

question: Trace young Fortinbras's military campaign in order: what was his original aim, how did old Norway redirect it, and what is his army doing when it crosses Denmark?
expected_answer: First Fortinbras gathered lawless resolutes to recover by force the lands his father lost to King Hamlet; then old Norway rebuked him, and gave him three thousand crowns and a commission to employ those soldiers against Poland instead; finally his army marches across Denmark under a promised safe-conduct on its way to Poland.

1.  **Original Aim:** Fortinbras originally raised levies (soldiers) with the aim of preparing against Poland. However, upon closer inspection, it was discovered that his true target was the King of Denmark ("your Highness").
    *   *Evidence:* `[act02_scene02_chunk005]` ("...preparation 'gainst the Polack; / But better look'd into, he truly found / It was against your Highness...")

2.  **Redirection by Old Norway:** When the King of Norway (Fortinbras's uncle) learned of this, he sent out arrests against Fortinbras. Fortinbras obeyed, received a rebuke, and vowed never to attack the Danish King again. Consequently, old Norway redirected the soldiers to fight against Poland instead.
    *   *Evidence:* `[act02_scene02_chunk005]` ("...sends out arrests / On Fortinbras; which he, in brief, obeys, / Receives rebuke from Norway; and in fine, / Makes vow before his uncle never more / To give th'assay of arms against your Majesty. / ... Gives him three thousand crowns in annual fee, / And his commission to employ those soldiers / So levied as before, against the Polack")

3.  **Current Activity Crossing Denmark:** When Fortinbras's army crosses Denmark, they are marching to a specific rendezvous point to convey a request to the Danish King for permission to march over his kingdom.
    *   *Evidence:* `[act04_scene04_chunk001]` ("...Craves the conveyance of a promis'd march / Over his kingdom. You know the rendezvous.")

question: Trace young Fortinbras's military campaign in order: what was his original aim, how did old Norway redirect it, and what is his army doing when it crosses Denmark?
expected_answer: First Fortinbras gathered lawless resolutes to recover by force the lands his father lost to King Hamlet; then old Norway rebuked him, and gave him three thousand crowns and a commission to employ those soldiers against Poland instead; finally his army marches across Denmark under a promised safe-conduct on its way to Poland.

1.  **Original Aim:** Fortinbras originally raised levies (soldiers) with the aim of preparing against Poland. However, upon closer inspection, it was discovered that his true target was the King of Denmark ("your Highness").
    *   *Evidence:* `[act02_scene02_chunk005]` ("...preparation 'gainst the Polack; / But better look'd into, he truly found / It was against your Highness...")

2.  **Redirection by Old Norway:** When the King of Norway (Fortinbras's uncle) learned of this, he sent out arrests against Fortinbras. Fortinbras obeyed, received a rebuke, and vowed never to attack the Danish King again. Consequently, old Norway redirected the soldiers to fight against Poland instead.
    *   *Evidence:* `[act02_scene02_chunk005]` ("...sends out arrests / On Fortinbras; which he, in brief, obeys, / Receives rebuke from Norway; and in fine, / Makes vow before his uncle never more / To give th'assay of arms against your Majesty. / ... Gives him three thousand crowns in annual fee, / And his commission to employ those soldiers / So levied as before, against the Polack")

3.  **Current Activity Crossing Denmark:** When Fortinbras's army crosses Denmark, they are marching to a specific rendezvous point to convey a request to the Danish King for permission to march over his kingdom.
    *   *Evidence:* `[act04_scene04_chunk001]` ("...Craves the conveyance of a promis'd march / Over his kingdom. You know the rendezvous.")

raw_chunks.text:
Gertrude, do not drink.

QUEEN.
I will, my lord; I pray you pardon me.

KING.
[_Aside._] It is the poison’d cup; it is too late.

HAMLET.
I dare not drink yet, madam. By and by.

QUEEN.
Come, let me wipe thy face.

LAERTES.
My lord, I’ll hit him now.

KING.
I do not think’t.

LAERTES.
[_Aside._] And yet ’tis almost ’gainst my conscience.

HAMLET.
Come for the third, Laertes. You do but dally.
I pray you pass with your best violence.
I am afeard you make a wanton of me.

LAERTES.
Say you so? Come on.

[_They play._]

OSRIC.
Nothing neither way.

LAERTES.
Have at you now.

[_Laertes wounds Hamlet; then, in scuffling, they change rapiers, and
Hamlet wounds Laertes._]

KING.
Part them; they are incens’d.

HAMLET.
Nay

raw_chunks.text:
Gertrude, do not drink.

QUEEN.
I will, my lord; I pray you pardon me.

KING.
[_Aside._] It is the poison’d cup; it is too late.

HAMLET.
I dare not drink yet, madam. By and by.

QUEEN.
Come, let me wipe thy face.

LAERTES.
My lord, I’ll hit him now.

KING.
I do not think’t.

LAERTES.
[_Aside._] And yet ’tis almost ’gainst my conscience.

HAMLET.
Come for the third, Laertes. You do but dally.
I pray you pass with your best violence.
I am afeard you make a wanton of me.

LAERTES.
Say you so? Come on.

[_They play._]

OSRIC.
Nothing neither way.

LAERTES.
Have at you now.

[_Laertes wounds Hamlet; then, in scuffling, they change rapiers, and
Hamlet wounds Laertes._]

KING.
Part them; they are incens’d.

HAMLET.
Nay

Nothing neither way.

LAERTES.
Have at you now.

[_Laertes wounds Hamlet; then, in scuffling, they change rapiers, and
Hamlet wounds Laertes._]

KING.
Part them; they are incens’d.

HAMLET.
Nay, come again!

[_The Queen falls._]

OSRIC.
Look to the Queen there, ho!

HORATIO.
They bleed on both sides. How is it, my lord?

OSRIC.
How is’t, Laertes?

LAERTES.
Why, as a woodcock to my own springe, Osric.
I am justly kill’d with mine own treachery.

HAMLET.
How does the Queen?

KING.
She swoons to see them bleed.

QUEEN.
No, no, the drink, the drink! O my dear Hamlet!
The drink, the drink! I am poison’d.

[_Dies._]

HAMLET.
O villany! Ho! Let the door be lock’d:
Treachery! Seek it out.

[_Laertes falls._]

LAERTES.
It is

Context chunks:
[act05_scene02_chunk021 | Act 5 Scene 2 | A hall in the Castle. | 256 tokens]
Gertrude, do not drink.

QUEEN.
I will, my lord; I pray you pardon me.

KING.
[_Aside._] It is the poison’d cup; it is too late.

HAMLET.
I dare not drink yet, madam. By and by.

QUEEN.
Come, let me wipe thy face.

LAERTES.
My lord, I’ll hit him now.

KING.
I do not think’t.

LAERTES.
[_Aside._] And yet ’tis almost ’gainst my conscience.

HAMLET.
Come for the third, Laertes. You do but dally.
I pray you pass with your best violence.
I am afeard you make a wanton of me.

LAERTES.
Say you so? Come on.

[_They play._]

OSRIC.
Nothing neither way.

LAERTES.
Have at you now.

[_Laertes wounds Hamlet; then, in scuffling, they change rapiers, and
Hamlet wounds Laertes._]

KING.
Part them; they are incens’d.

HAMLET.
Nay

[act05_scene02_chunk023 | Act 5 Scene 2 | A hall in the Castle. | 256 tokens]
my dear Hamlet!
The drink, the drink! I am poison’d.

[_Dies._]

HAMLET.
O villany! Ho! Let the door be lock’d:
Treachery! Seek it out.

[_Laertes falls._]

LAERTES.
It is here, Hamlet. Hamlet, thou art slain.
No medicine in the world can do thee good.
In thee there is not half an hour of life;
The treacherous instrument is in thy hand,
Unbated and envenom’d. The foul practice
Hath turn’d itself on me. Lo, here I lie,
Never to rise again. Thy mother’s poison’d.
I can no more. The King, the King’s to blame.

HAMLET.
The point envenom’d too!
Then, venom, to thy work.

[_Stabs the King._]

OSRIC and LORDS.
Treason! treason!

KING.
O yet defend me, friends. I am but hurt.

HAMLET.
Here, thou incestuous, murderous, damned Dane,
Drink off this potion. Is thy union here?
Follow my mother

QUEEN.
I will, my lord; I pray you pardon me.

I dare not drink yet, madam. By and by.

QUEEN.
Come, let me wipe thy face.

LAERTES.
My lord, I’ll hit him now.

KING.
I do not think’t.

LAERTES.
[_Aside._] And yet ’tis almost ’gainst my conscience.

HAMLET.
Come for the third, Laertes. You do but dally.
I pray you pass with your best violence.
I am afeard you make a wanton of me.

Say you so? Come on.

Nothing neither way.

my dear Hamlet!
The drink, the drink! I am poison’d.

[_Dies._]

HAMLET.
O villany! Ho! Let the door be lock’d:
Treachery! Seek it out.

[_Laertes falls._]

LAERTES.
It is here, Hamlet. Hamlet, thou art slain.
No medicine in the world can do thee good.
In thee there is not half an hour of life;
The treacherous instrument is in thy hand,
Unbated and envenom’d. The foul practice
Hath turn’d itself on me. Lo, here I lie,
Never to rise again. Thy mother’s poison’d.
I can no more. The King, the King’s to blame.

HAMLET.
The point envenom’d too!
Then, venom, to thy work.

[_Stabs the King._]

OSRIC and LORDS.
Treason! treason!

KING.
O yet defend me, friends. I am but hurt.

HAMLET.
Here, thou incestuous, murderous, damned Dane,
Drink off this potion. Is thy union here?
Follow my mother

Based on the provided context, the four characters who die onstage in the final scene (Act 5, Scene 2) and the causes of their deaths are:

1.  **Queen Gertrude**: She dies from poison.
    *   **Evidence**: The text states, "The Queen falls," followed by her exclamation, "No, no, the drink, the drink! O my dear Hamlet! The drink, the drink! I am poison'd." She then dies.
    *   **Chunk ID**: `act05_scene02_chunk022`

2.  **King Claudius**: He dies from poison administered by Hamlet.
    *   **Evidence**: Hamlet forces him to drink, saying, "Drink off this potion." Immediately after, the King says, "O yet defend me, friends. I am but hurt," and then the text indicates his death with the stage direction "[King dies._]" (though the specific line "I am but hurt" precedes the death confirmation in the flow of the scene described).
    *   **Chunk ID**: `act05_scene02_chunk024`

3.  **Laertes**: He dies from the poison in his own rapier wound (which he used to wound Hamlet).
    *   **Evidence**: Laertes says, "Why, as a woodcock to my own springe, Osric. I am justly kill'd with mine own treachery." Later, he confirms the nature of the poison: "It is a poison temper'd by himself."
    *   **Chunk ID**: `act05_scene02_chunk022` and `act05_scene02_chunk024`

4.  **Hamlet**: He dies from the poison in his own wound (inflicted by Laertes).
    *   **Evidence**: While the specific moment of his death is not explicitly detailed with a "dies" stage direction in the provided snippets, the context confirms he is mortally wounded ("They bleed on both sides," "Mine and my father's death come not upon thee") and delivers his final lines, "I am dead, Horatio," before the scene ends.
    *   **Chunk ID**: `act05_scene02_chunk022` and `act05_scene02_chunk024`

**Summary of Causes:**
*   **Gertrude**: Poison

model_output:
Based on the provided context, the four characters who die onstage in the final scene (Act 5, Scene 2) and the causes of their deaths are:

1.  **Queen Gertrude**: She dies from poison in a drink.
    *   *Evidence:* "The Queen falls." followed by her exclamation, "No, no, the drink, the drink! O my dear Hamlet! The drink, the drink! I am poison'd." and her subsequent death.
    *   *Chunk ID:* `act05_scene02_chunk022`

2.  **Laertes**: He dies from a wound inflicted by Hamlet with an envenomed rapier (which he originally intended for Hamlet).
    *   *Evidence:* Laertes states, "I am justly kill'd with mine own treachery," and later, "The treacherous instrument is in thy hand, Unbated and envenom'd." He also says, "Never to rise again."
    *   *Chunk ID:* `act05_scene02_chunk022` and `act05_scene02_chunk023`

3.  **King Claudius**: He dies from the poison in the cup Hamlet forces him to drink.
    *   *Evidence:* Hamlet says to him, "Drink off this potion." Claudius responds, "O yet defend me, friends. I am but hurt," before dying (implied by the context of the scene ending and the command to follow his mother).
    *   *Chunk ID:* `act05_scene02_chunk023`

4.  **Hamlet**: He dies from the wound inflicted by Laertes with the envenomed rapier.
    *   *Evidence:* Laertes tells him, "Hamlet, thou art slain. No medicine in the world can do thee good. In thee there is not half an hour of life."
    *   *Chunk ID:* `act05_scene02_chunk023`

**Cited Chunk IDs:**
*   `act05_scene02_chunk022`
*   `act05_scene02_chunk023`

SCENE IV. Another room in the Castle.

Enter Queen and Polonius.

POLONIUS.
He will come straight. Look you lay home to him,
Tell him his pranks have been too broad to bear with,
And that your Grace hath screen’d and stood between
Much heat and him. I’ll silence me e’en here.
Pray you be round with him.

HAMLET.
[_Within._] Mother, mother, mother.

QUEEN.
I’ll warrant you, Fear me not.
Withdraw, I hear him coming.

[_Polonius goes behind the arras._]

Enter Hamlet.

HAMLET.
Now, mother, what’s the matter?

QUEEN.
Hamlet, thou hast thy father much offended.

HAMLET.
Mother, you have my father much offended.

QUEEN.
Come, come, you answer with an idle tongue.

QUEEN.
Why, how now, Hamlet?

What’s the matter now?

QUEEN.
Have you forgot me?

HAMLET.

HAMLET.
A bloody deed. Almost as bad, good mother,

As kill a king?

model_output:
Based on the provided context, when Hamlet stabs through the arras, he thinks he may have killed **the King** (referring to the "rat" he heard calling for help), but **Polonius** is actually killed.

**Evidence:**
*   **Who Hamlet thinks he killed:** In chunk `act03_scene04_chunk003`, after stabbing, Hamlet asks, "Nay, I know not. Is it the King?" This indicates his suspicion that the person behind the tapestry was the King.
*   **Who was actually killed:** In chunk `act03_scene04_chunk002`, immediately after Hamlet makes the pass through the arras, Polonius cries out, "O, I am slain!" and subsequently falls and dies.

question: What two reasons does Claudius give Laertes for not publicly punishing Hamlet?
expected_answer: He says Gertrude is deeply attached to Hamlet, and the public loves Hamlet so much that punishment would turn against Claudius.

required_evidence_quotes.quote:

raw_chunks.text:
’s so conjunctive to my life and soul,
That, as the star moves not but in his sphere,
I could not but by her. The other motive,
Why to a public count I might not go,
Is the great love the general gender bear him,
Who, dipping all his faults in their affection,
Would like the spring that turneth wood to stone,
Convert his gyves to graces; so that my arrows,
Too slightly timber’d for so loud a wind,
Would have reverted to my bow again,
And not where I had aim’d them.

LAERTES.
And so have I a noble father lost,
A sister driven into desperate terms,
Whose worth, if praises may go back again,
Stood challenger on mount of all the age
For her perfections. But my revenge will come.

KING.
Break not your sleeps for that. You must not think
That we are made of stuff so flat and dull
That we can let our beard be shook with danger,
And think it pastime. You shortly shall hear more.
I lov’d your father, and we love ourself,
And that, I hope, will teach

Pursu’d my life.

My virtue or my plague, be it either which,—

’s so conjunctive to my life and soul,
That, as the star moves not but in his sphere,
I could not but by her. The other motive,
Why to a public count I might not go,
Is the great love the general gender bear him,
Who, dipping all his faults in their affection,
Would like the spring that turneth wood to stone,
Convert his gyves to graces; so that my arrows,
Too slightly timber’d for so loud a wind,
Would have reverted to my bow again,
And not where I had aim’d them.

LAERTES.
And so have I a noble father lost,
A sister driven into desperate terms,
Whose worth, if praises may go back again,
Stood challenger on mount of all the age
For her perfections. But my revenge will come.

KING.
Break not your sleeps for that. You must not think
That we are made of stuff so flat and dull
That we can let our beard be shook with danger,
And think it pastime. You shortly shall hear more.
I lov’d your father, and we love ourself,
And that, I hope, will teach

model_output:
Based on the provided context, Claudius gives Laertes two reasons for not publicly punishing Hamlet:

1.  **The Queen's influence:** Claudius states that Queen Gertrude ("The Queen his mother") lives almost entirely by Hamlet's looks and is so connected to his life and soul that he could not act against Hamlet without her consent ("I could not but by her").
2.  **Public opinion:** Claudius explains that the general public holds great love for Hamlet. He argues that if he were to publicly accuse Hamlet, the people would overlook Hamlet's faults due to their affection, effectively turning his punishment into a grace ("Convert his gyves to graces"). Consequently, Claudius's accusations would fail to reach their intended target ("Would have reverted to my bow again").

**Cited Chunk IDs:**
*   `act04_scene07_chunk001` (Contains the introduction of the first reason regarding the Queen and the beginning of the second reason regarding public affection).
*   `act04_scene07_chunk002` (Contains the completion of the second reason regarding the public's ability to convert faults into graces).

KING.

’s so conjunctive to my life and soul,

Who, dipping all his faults in their affection,
Would like the spring that turneth wood to stone,

LAERTES.

model_output:
Based on the provided context, Claudius gives Laertes two reasons for not publicly punishing Hamlet:

1.  **The Queen's influence:** Claudius states that Queen Gertrude ("The Queen his mother") lives almost entirely by Hamlet's looks and is so connected to his life and soul that he could not act against Hamlet without her consent ("I could not but by her").
2.  **Public opinion:** Claudius explains that the general public holds great love for Hamlet. He argues that if he were to publicly accuse Hamlet, the people would overlook Hamlet's faults due to their affection, effectively turning his punishment into a grace ("Convert his gyves to graces"). Consequently, Claudius's accusations would fail to reach their intended target ("Would have reverted to my bow again").

**Cited Chunk IDs:**
*   `act04_scene07_chunk001` (Contains the introduction of the first reason regarding the Queen and the beginning of the second reason regarding public affection).
*   `act04_scene07_chunk002` (Contains the completion of the second reason regarding the public's ability to convert faults into graces).

HAMLET.
Whose was it?

FIRST CLOWN.
A whoreson, mad fellow’s it was. Whose do you think it was?

HAMLET.
Nay, I know not.

A pestilence on him for a mad rogue! A pour’d a flagon of Rhenish on my

HAMLET.
This?

FIRST CLOWN.
E’en that.

HAMLET.

see. [_Takes the skull._] Alas, poor Yorick. I knew him,
Horatio, a fellow of infinite jest, of most excellent fancy. He hath
borne me on his back a thousand times; and now, how abhorred in my
imagination it is! My gorge rises at it. Here hung those lips that I
have kiss’d I know not how oft. Where be your gibes now? your gambols?
your songs? your flashes of merriment, that were wont to set the table
on a roar? Not one now, to mock your own grinning? Quite chop-fallen?
Now get you to my lady’s chamber, and tell her, let her paint an inch
thick, to this favour she must come. Make her laugh at that.—Prithee,
Horatio, tell me one thing.

HORATIO.
What’s that, my lord?

HAMLET.
Dost thou think Alexander looked o’ this fashion i’ th’earth?

HORATIO.
E’en so.

HAMLET.
And smelt so? Pah!

[_Throws down the skull._]

HORATIO.
E’en so, my lord

CLOWN.
Why, sir, his hide is so tann’d with his trade that he will keep out
water a great while. And your water is a sore decayer of your whoreson
dead body. Here’s a skull now; this skull hath lain in the earth
three-and-twenty years.

HAMLET.
Whose was it?

FIRST CLOWN.
A whoreson, mad fellow’s it was. Whose do you think it was?

HAMLET.
Nay, I know not.

FIRST CLOWN.
A pestilence on him for a mad rogue! A pour’d a flagon of Rhenish on my
head once. This same skull, sir, was Yorick’s skull, the King’s jester.

HAMLET.
This?

FIRST CLOWN.
E’en that.

HAMLET.
Let me see. [_Takes the skull._] Alas, poor Yorick. I knew him,

[act05_scene01_chunk014 | Act 5 Scene 1 | A churchyard. | 256 tokens]
see. [_Takes the skull._] Alas, poor Yorick. I knew him,
Horatio, a fellow of infinite jest, of most excellent fancy. He hath
borne me on his back a thousand times; and now, how abhorred in my
imagination it is! My gorge rises at it. Here hung those lips that I
have kiss’d I know not how oft. Where be your gibes now? your gambols?
your songs? your flashes of merriment, that were wont to set the table
on a roar? Not one now, to mock your own grinning? Quite chop-fallen?
Now get you to my lady’s chamber, and tell her, let her paint an inch
thick, to this favour she must come. Make her laugh at that.—Prithee,
Horatio, tell me one thing.

HORATIO.
What’s that, my lord?

HAMLET.
Dost thou think Alexander looked o’ this fashion i’ th’earth?

HORATIO.
E’en so.

HAMLET.
And smelt so? Pah!

[_Throws down the skull._]

HORATIO.
E’en so, my lord

see. [_Takes the skull._] Alas, poor Yorick. I knew him,

E’en so.
